components.name: .Building Blocks/Active track
components.description: A visual indicator displaying the current progress or active step within a sequential process or timeline.
components.usage: Apply to highlight the present position in multi-step flows, progress indicators, or step navigators. Use when users need to understand where they are in a linear journey and what remains ahead.
components.never: Don't use for decorative purposes or to indicate completion status — use a completed state variant or checkmark component instead.

components.name: .Building Blocks/Discrete dots
components.description: A series of individual dots that represent distinct, non-continuous states or steps.
components.usage: Use discrete dots to indicate progress through a finite sequence of steps, show current position in a paginated set, or represent individual selection states. Each dot should map to one discrete item or milestone, not a continuous range.
components.never: Don't use for continuous progress representation — use a progress bar instead. Discrete dots are for countable, distinct states only.

components.name: .Building Blocks/Handle
components.description: A draggable affordance that signals an element can be moved or reordered within a list or container.
components.usage: Apply to the left edge of list items, table rows, or card groups where users need to manually reorder content. Pair with drag-and-drop interaction patterns. The handle provides visual confirmation that an item is draggable without requiring additional text labels.
components.never: Don't use as a general interactive element or button — use Button or IconButton instead. Handles are only for indicating drag capability.

components.name: .Building Blocks/Inactive track left
components.description: A non-interactive left-aligned track segment that serves as a background or unfilled state in progress indicators and range inputs.
components.usage: Use as the unfilled portion of a linear progress bar, slider track, or stepped indicator. Pair with an active track variant to show completion state. Apply when the visual hierarchy requires a muted, disabled, or pending segment.
components.never: Don't use for interactive elements — use the active track variant or Button instead. The inactive state must never respond to user input.

components.name: .Building Blocks/Inactive track right
components.description: A visual indicator representing a disabled or completed step in a multi-step process, positioned on the right side of a sequence.
components.usage: Use in stepper or progress components to mark steps that are unavailable or already completed. Place it between active steps and future steps to establish clear visual hierarchy. Pair with active and completed track variants to create a coherent progression visualization.
components.never: Don't use for interactive elements — inactive tracks are read-only status indicators. Use interactive button or link components if users need to navigate to previous steps.

components.name: .Building Blocks/Value indicator
components.description: A visual indicator that displays a quantitative value with semantic color coding to represent its status or magnitude.
components.usage: Use to highlight numeric metrics, KPIs, or status values that require immediate visual recognition. Apply when the value's significance depends on color differentiation (positive/negative/neutral states). Pair with accompanying labels or context to ensure clarity of what is being measured.
components.never: Don't use for pure numeric display without context — pair with a descriptive label or metric name so users understand what value is being shown.

components.name: .Clock face - 12 hour
components.description: A 12-hour analog clock face displaying hours and minutes with numbered hour markers and rotating hand indicators.
components.usage: Use when representing time in analog format where users benefit from visual time estimation or contextual time display. Suitable for scheduling interfaces, time pickers, and educational contexts. Pair with digital time displays when precise minute reading is critical.
components.never: Don't use as the only time input method in forms—users cannot reliably input specific times with analog interfaces. Provide a digital input field as an alternative.

components.name: .Clock face - 24 hour
components.description: A 24-hour circular clock face displaying time in military format with hour and minute indicators.
components.usage: Use for applications requiring precise time selection or display in 24-hour format, such as scheduling tools, time pickers, or aviation/military interfaces. Appropriate when users need to distinguish between AM/PM equivalents without additional context.
components.never: Don't use for general consumer time displays — use a 12-hour clock face with AM/PM unless the 24-hour format is explicitly required by your domain.

components.name: .Header
components.description: A top-level container that establishes visual hierarchy and contains primary navigation, branding, and global actions.
components.usage: Use Header as the persistent top bar of your application. Place primary navigation items, logo, and account controls within it. Header should span the full viewport width and remain accessible when scrolling.
components.never: Don't use Header for page-specific content — reserve it for global application chrome. Don't nest multiple Headers vertically.

components.name: 3D Avatars / 1

components.name: 3D Avatars / 9

components.name: Assistive chip
components.description: Assist chips represent smart or automated actions that can span multiple apps. They should appear dynamically and contextually in a UI.

components.name: Badge
components.description: Badges are used to indicate a notification, item count, or other information relating to a navigation destination. They are placed on the ending edge of icons, typically within other components.

components.name: Basic dialog
components.description:
A Dialog is a modal window that appears in front of app content to provide critical information or ask for a decision. They disable all app functionality when they appear, and remain on screen until a required action has been taken.

Being purposefully interruptive they should be used sparingly. To provide options without interrupting a user’s experience use a Menu.

components.name: Bottom app bar/4
components.description: A bottom app bar is a fixed container at the viewport bottom that provides primary actions and navigation within a single screen.
components.usage: Use when you need persistent access to 1-4 primary actions without consuming horizontal space. Typically paired with a FAB for the main action. Reserve for mobile-first interfaces where the bottom position aligns with thumb-reachable navigation zones.
components.never: Don't use for secondary or contextual actions — the bottom app bar commits prime real estate and should host only the most critical controls for the current screen.

components.name: Bottom sheet
components.description:
Bottom sheets are surfaces containing supplementary content,  anchored to the bottom of the screen.

🛠 Additional options 
Drag handle - can be toggled to hide or show

components.name: Building Blocks / Headline
components.description: A semantic heading element that establishes content hierarchy and structure with configurable typography levels.
components.usage: Apply Headline to mark primary section titles and major content divisions. Select the appropriate level (H1–H6) based on document hierarchy, not visual size. Use a single H1 per page to establish primary topic context.
components.never: Don't use Headline for styling generic text — use Body or Caption instead. Heading levels must follow document structure sequentially; skipping levels (H1 to H3) breaks assistive technology navigation.

components.name: Building Blocks / Nav Item
components.description: Navigation drawer building blocks

components.name: Building Blocks / Section Header
components.description: A typographic container that introduces and visually separates a content section with hierarchical emphasis.
components.usage: Use to mark the beginning of distinct content sections within a page or container, establishing visual hierarchy and improving scannability. Apply consistent spacing above and below to create clear content rhythm. Reserve for primary section divisions; use smaller heading levels for subsections.
components.never: Don't use multiple section headers consecutively without content between them — this breaks visual hierarchy and confuses users about content organization.

components.name: Building Blocks / segment
components.description: Navigation Bar segment building blocks

components.name: Building blocks / Snackbar-action
components.description: A pressable action button nested within a snackbar that triggers the snackbar's primary action.
components.usage: Place inside a snackbar to provide a single, contextual action for users to take in response to the message. The action button should have a short label (one to two words) that clearly describes the outcome. Reserve this for critical user interactions that require immediate response rather than dismissal alone.
components.never: Don't use snackbar actions for navigation — use a snackbar with a Link component or dismiss and navigate separately instead.

components.name: Building blocks / Snackbar-close-affordance
components.description: A close button affordance for dismissing snackbar notifications, with inverse color treatment and state-specific visual feedback.
components.usage: Place within snackbar components to provide users with explicit dismiss capability. Use when snackbar content requires user action to remove, rather than auto-dismissal. The inverse styling ensures sufficient contrast against snackbar backgrounds.
components.never: Don't use outside of snackbar containers — the inverse color scheme is calibrated specifically for snackbar surfaces and will have insufficient contrast elsewhere.

components.name: Building Blocks/ Carousel-item-text
components.description:
Carousel items are primarily visual. Item text is optional and should be brief. If your carousel items require a lot of text, consider using a series of cards instead. Text should always be understandable at each size. Consider adapting the text to use brief labels on smaller carousel items.

🛠️
Supporting text visibility can be turned on and off in the properties panel

components.name: Building Blocks/button segment (middle)
components.description: Segment button used in the middle of segment button groups

components.name: Building blocks/Card states/Outlined
components.description: A contained surface with border, elevation, or fill treatment that responds to interaction states.
components.usage: Use outlined cards as lightweight containers for grouped content when visual separation is needed without strong emphasis. Choose elevated cards for content requiring hierarchy prominence, and filled cards when maximum contrast is required. All variants support enabled, hovered, focused, pressed, and dragged states.
components.never: Don't use card states to trigger navigation—wrap cards in a Link component or add a distinct button if interaction is required.

components.name: Building Blocks/Direct Input (keyboard) input
components.description: A text input field that captures user keyboard input with focus and interaction states.
components.usage: Use for capturing typed user input in forms, searches, or data entry. Choose the focused variant (true) to show active state styling during interaction, and the unfocused variant (false) for default/idle appearance. Apply appropriate token mappings based on context—use PrimaryContainer background for emphasized inputs or SurfaceContainerHighest for standard form fields.
components.never: Don't use for rich text editing or multi-line content—use a textarea component instead. This component is optimized for single-line keyboard input only.

components.name: Building Blocks/Hour
components.description: Hour line building block

components.name: Building Blocks/hour-line
components.description: A visual timeline marker representing a specific hour (1-24) in a 24-hour cycle.
components.usage: Use hour-line to display time-based schedules, calendars, or timeline views where hourly divisions need visual distinction. Each variant maps to its corresponding hour, enabling clear temporal organization in scheduling interfaces.
components.never: Don't use hour-line as a separator or divider between unrelated content—it's semantically tied to time representation and should only appear in temporal contexts.

components.name: Building Blocks/image-Thumbnail
components.description:
Image thumbnail 
-- 
Replace fill with Image Fill of your choice

components.name: Building Blocks/Input
components.description: A text input field that captures single-line user text entries with optional focus and active states.
components.usage: Use Input for collecting short text data like usernames, email addresses, or search queries. Pair with clear labels and error states to guide users. Choose the active variant (true) when the field requires immediate user interaction or validation feedback.
components.never: Don't use Input for multi-line content — use Textarea instead. Input enforces single-line behavior and will truncate or hide overflow text.

components.name: Building Blocks/Leading element
components.description: A visual container that precedes content to establish hierarchy, context, or visual emphasis through icon or indentation.
components.usage: Use the Icon variant to signal content type, status, or category at a glance. Apply the Indent variant when progressive disclosure or nested relationships require structural alignment without additional imagery.
components.never: Don't use as a clickable interactive element — pair with Button or Link components instead. Leading elements are decorative anchors, not inputs.

components.name: Building Blocks/Local M3 calendar cell
components.description: Day cell

components.name: Building blocks/Menu button
components.description: 🔍 Menu button

components.name: Building Blocks/Menu List Item
components.description: A single selectable item within a menu or list that responds to user interaction with visual state changes.
components.usage: Use for individual menu entries, list selections, or dropdown options where users need to identify and select a single item. Pair with a parent Menu or List container to manage selection state and keyboard navigation. Include an icon or text label to clearly identify each option.
components.never: Don't use as a standalone button — use Button component instead. Menu List Item requires a parent container to manage focus and selection semantics.

components.name: Building Blocks/Period Selector
components.description: Period selector building block

components.name: Building Blocks/Period Selector - Horizontal
components.description: Period selector building block

components.name: Building Blocks/Segment
components.description: Navigation rail segment

components.name: Building Blocks/state-layer/1. enabled
components.description: A visual layer that applies enabled state styling to interactive components.
components.usage: Apply this state layer when a component is active and ready for user interaction. Use it as the baseline visual state for buttons, inputs, and other interactive elements that are not disabled, focused, or pressed.
components.never: Don't use for disabled states — apply the disabled state layer instead to ensure proper visual and semantic communication of unavailable interactions.

components.name: Building Blocks/state-layer/2. hovered
components.description: A surface state layer that indicates hover interaction on interactive elements.
components.usage: Apply this layer when a user hovers over buttons, cards, list items, or other interactive surfaces to provide visual feedback. Use sparingly on dark backgrounds where the hover layer remains perceptible; pair with cursor changes for clarity on non-obvious interactive targets.
components.never: Don't apply hover state to non-interactive elements or static text — this misleads users about what can be clicked and violates interaction patterns.

components.name: Building Blocks/state-layer/3. focused
components.description: A focus state layer that provides visual feedback when a surface element receives keyboard or screen reader focus.
components.usage: Apply this layer to interactive components that accept focus to meet WCAG 2.1 Level AA focus visibility requirements. Use when a component must clearly indicate focus state independently of color alone, particularly for low-contrast backgrounds.
components.never: Don't use as a standalone visual element or decorative overlay — it exists exclusively to communicate focus state and should only render when focus is actually present.

components.name: Building Blocks/state-layer/4. pressed
components.description: A state layer that applies a pressed visual treatment to interactive surfaces.
components.usage: Apply this layer when a user actively presses or clicks an interactive element. Use it to provide immediate tactile feedback that distinguishes the pressed state from hover or focus states. Ensure the pressed state is distinct enough to be perceived as a separate interaction phase.
components.never: Don't use pressed state as a substitute for disabled state — disabled elements require their own distinct styling to indicate they cannot be interacted with.

components.name: Building Blocks/state-layer/5. dragged
components.description: A visual state layer that indicates an element is being dragged, applied as an overlay to the surface it affects.
components.usage: Apply when a user initiates a drag operation to provide immediate visual feedback that the element is active and in motion. Use this state throughout the drag sequence until the user releases the element. This state layer works in concert with cursor feedback and should appear on the dragged element itself, not on potential drop targets.
components.never: Don't apply the drag state to drop target zones — use a dedicated drop-zone highlight instead. Drag state is for the element being moved, not the destination.

components.name: Building Blocks/Tab Single Unit/Primary/Icon and label
components.description: Primary tabs label and icon building blocks

components.name: Building Blocks/Trailing element
components.description: A flexible container positioned at the end of a parent component that displays supplementary content through icon or text variants.
components.usage: Use trailing elements to provide secondary actions, status indicators, or metadata that contextually complements the primary content. Choose the icon variant for compact visual indicators and the text variant for brief labels, counts, or badges. Position it within list items, cards, or input fields where space permits right-aligned content.
components.never: Don't use for primary actions — trailing elements are supplementary; if the action is critical to the user's task, place it in the primary content area or use a dedicated Button component.

components.name: Building Blocks/video-Thumbnail
components.description:
Image thumbnail 
-- 
Replace fill with Image Fill of your choice

components.name: Building Blocks/Year
components.description: Year cell

components.name: Button
components.description: Buttons communicate actions that users can take. They are typically placed throughout your UI, in places like Dialogs, Modal windows, Forms, Cards and Toolbars

props.name: variant
props.description: Visual style variant that determines the button's appearance and emphasis level

props.name: disabled
props.description: When true, prevents user interaction and applies disabled styling

props.name: onClick
props.description: Callback function invoked when the button is clicked or activated

props.name: children
props.description: Button label text or content displayed inside the button

props.name: type
props.description: HTML button type attribute for form handling behavior

components.usage: Use buttons to trigger actions or navigation within your application. Place buttons in consistent locations like form submissions, dialog confirmations, or contextual actions within cards and toolbars. Choose variant based on visual hierarchy: Filled for primary actions, Outlined for secondary actions, Text for tertiary actions, Tonal for medium emphasis, and Elevated for raised emphasis on low-contrast surfaces.
components.never: Never use more than one Filled button per context or section as it reduces visual hierarchy. Never use buttons for navigation that should be handled by links - buttons trigger actions while links navigate to new pages. Never disable buttons without explanation - provide clear feedback about why an action is unavailable.
components.compose: Buttons can be composed with icons for enhanced clarity, but maintain accessible text labels. Group related buttons together with consistent spacing, placing primary actions on the right in LTR layouts.

aria.keyboard: Space or Enter key activates the button. Tab moves focus to the button. Disabled buttons cannot receive keyboard focus.
aria.screenReader: Announces the button's label text, role as button, and disabled state if applicable. Pressed state announced for toggle buttons.

components.name: Card
components.description: Cards are used to display content and actions on a single topic. They should be easy to scan for relevant and actionable information. Cards can be displayed together in a grid, vertical list, or carousel.

components.name: Carousel
components.description:
A Carousel contains a collection of items that can be scrolled on and off the screen.

🛠️
Select between Uniform and Non-uniform layouts;
Choose Mobile or Tablet context
An optional item-text’s visibility can be toggled on and off

components.name: Carousel - Full screen
components.description:
A Carousel contains a collection of items that can be scrolled on and off the screen.

🛠️
Select between Uniform and Non-uniform layouts;
Choose Mobile or Tablet context
An optional item-text’s visibility can be toggled on and off

components.name: Checkboxes
components.description: Checkboxes are a selection control which allow users to select one or more options from a set; present a list containing sub-selections; and turn an item on or off in a desktop environment

components.name: Device/Device frame
components.description: A container that represents the physical frame and viewport of a device, with light and dark theme variants.
components.usage: Use Device frame to showcase responsive designs, mobile mockups, or app interfaces within their native device context. Choose the light variant for bright backgrounds and dark variant for dark contexts. Apply this when demonstrating how content appears on specific device types.
components.never: Don't use Device frame as a generic card or content wrapper — reserve it for actual device presentation contexts where the physical device representation adds communicative value.

components.name: Device/Device frame components/navigation
components.description: A navigation component that provides contextual wayfinding and hierarchical structure within device frame contexts.
components.usage: Use when establishing primary or secondary navigation patterns within mockups, prototypes, or device frame layouts. Apply to represent mobile app navigation bars, desktop sidebar navigation, or tab-based navigation systems that require visual hierarchy and state management.
components.never: Don't use for breadcrumb trails — use a dedicated Breadcrumb component instead. Navigation components manage entire navigation structures; breadcrumbs communicate location within a hierarchy.

components.name: Device/Device frame components/status bar
components.description: A status bar is the top system UI element that displays device status indicators, time, and notification icons.
components.usage: Use status bar to represent authentic device frames in design previews, prototypes, or educational contexts showing mobile interfaces. Include it when demonstrating full-screen mobile layouts to establish visual context and device authenticity.
components.never: Don't make the status bar interactive or clickable — it's a presentational frame element that should remain visually inert to avoid confusion with actual app controls.

components.name: Dial picker
components.description:
Time pickers help users select and set a specific time.

🛠 Additional options
Time picker input - Edit the selection of the input by selecting the individual input and editing the variant properties.

Period selector - Change between AM or PM as well as states by selecting the selector and editing the variant properties.

Hour Line - Change angle of hour and time highlighted by selecting the hour line and editing the variant property.

components.name: Divider/horizontal
components.description: A horizontal line that separates content sections or groups within a page.
components.usage: Use to create visual hierarchy between distinct content blocks, list items, or form sections. Choose inset variants when the divider should respect layout margins; use full-width for maximum visual separation. Apply the subhead variant to label grouped sections.
components.never: Don't use multiple dividers in sequence without content between them — this creates visual noise and undermines the separation purpose.

components.name: Divider/vertical
components.description: A vertical line that separates content sections or groups within a layout.
components.usage: Use to create visual hierarchy between adjacent content columns or to delineate logical groupings in horizontal layouts. The inset variants reduce visual weight for secondary separations, while full-width creates stronger structural divisions. Choose middle-inset for balanced spacing around the divider.
components.never: Don't use multiple vertical dividers in rapid succession — this fragments the layout. Use a grid or flexbox gap instead.

components.name: Docked Input Date Picker [desktop]
components.description:
Date pickers let users select a date, or a range of dates.

🛠 Additional options 
Days/Years - edit the states of the days/years by selecting the individual day/year and editing the variant properties.

Menu button - edit menu button states by selecting and editing variant properties.

components.name: Examples/Menu with Text field - Example 1
components.description:
This is an example of the Menu in use. Menus display a list of choices on a temporary surface. They appear when users interact with a button, action, or other control.  

🛠 Additional options 
Menu list item - can be edited by selecting individual list item and  selecting wether it is enabled, selected, and has a divider from the variant properties.

components.name: Examples/Menu with Text field - Example 2
components.description:
This is an example of the Menu in use. Menus display a list of choices on a temporary surface. They appear when users interact with a button, action, or other control.  

🛠 Additional options 
Menu list item - can be edited by selecting individual list item and  selecting wether it is enabled, selected, and has a divider from the variant properties.

components.name: Filter chip
components.description:
Filter chips use tags or descriptive words to filter content. They can be a good alternative to toggle buttons or checkboxes.

Tapping on a filter chip activates it and appends a leading checkmark icon to the starting edge of the chip label.

components.name: Icon button
components.description:
Icon buttons display actions in a compact layout. Icon buttons can represent opening actions such as opening an overflow menu or search, or represent binary actions that can be toggled on and off, such as favorite or bookmark.

Icon buttons can be grouped together or they can stand alone.

components.name: Icon button toggleable
components.description:
Icon buttons display actions in a compact layout. Icon buttons can represent opening actions such as opening an overflow menu or search, or represent binary actions that can be toggled on and off, such as favorite or bookmark.

Icon buttons can be grouped together or they can stand alone.

components.name: Input chip
components.description: Input chips represent discrete pieces of information entered by a user, such as Gmail contacts or filter options within a search field.

variants.name: Configuration=default, State=enabled, Selected=false, Show closing icon=true
Configuration=label & avatar, State=enabled, Selected=false, Show closing icon=true
Configuration=default, State=hovered, Selected=false, Show closing icon=true

components.name: Input Date Picker
components.description:
Date pickers let users select a date, or a range of dates.

🛠 Additional options 
Fields - edit the states of the fields by selecting the individual field and editing the variant properties.

components.name: Keyboard
components.description:
Use this Keyboard Component in your prototypes and wireframes. (This component is for placement only and is not an accurate representation of Android’s Gboard)

🛠
Toggle between different Configurations (Base keyboard, Alphanumeric, Keypad, Numeric only ) and Styles (Portrait, Landscape, Floating)

components.name: Keyboard picker
components.description:
Time pickers help users select and set a specific time.

🛠 Additional options
Time picker input - You can edit the selection of the input by selecting the individual input and editing the variant properties.

Period selector - You can change between AM or PM as well as states by selecting the selector and editing the variant properties.

components.name: List
components.description:
Lists are vertically organized groups of text and images (List Items).

Density can be used in denser UIs where space is limited. Density is only applied to the height.

🛠 Additional options 
List item - can be edited by selecting an individual list item and  selecting states, leading icons, trailing icons, and dividers from the variant properties.

components.name: List item/List Item: 0 Density
components.description:
List items can contain primary and supplemental actions represented by text, a leading icon and/or a trailing icon

Density can be used in denser UIs where space is limited. Density is only applied to the height.

🛠 Additional options 
List item - can be edited by selecting an individual list item and  selecting states, leading icons, trailing icons, and dividers from the variant properties.

components.name: Menu/0
components.description:
Menu: 0 Density 
Menus display a list of choices on a temporary surface. They appear when users interact with a button, action, or other control.

🛠 Additional options 
Menu list item - can be edited by selecting individual list item and  selecting wether it is enabled, selected, and has a divider from the variant properties.

components.name: Modal Date Picker
components.description:
Date pickers let users select a date, or a range of dates.

🛠 Additional options 
Days/Years - edit the states of the days/years by selecting the individual day/year and editing the variant properties.

Menu button - edit menu button states by selecting and editing variant properties.

components.name: Navigation bar
components.description:
Navigation bars (nav bars) provide access to three to five destinations.

The nav bar is positioned at the bottom of screens for convenient access. Each destination is represented by an icon and optional text label.
When a navigation bar icon is tapped or focused, the user is taken to the navigation destination associated with that icon. 

Use on mobile or tablet only.

🛠 Additional options 
Segments - can be edited by selecting individual segments and  selecting states, labels, and badges from the variant properties.

components.name: Navigation Drawer
components.description:
Navigation drawers provide access to destinations and app functionality, such as switching accounts. They can either be permanently on-screen or opened and closed by a navigation menu icon.

Navigation drawers are recommended for apps with 5 or more top-level destinations, apps with 2 or more levels of navigation hierarchy, quick navigation between unrelated destinations, or replacing the navigation rail or navigation bar on large screens.

🛠 Additional options 
Nav Item - Can be edited by selecting individual item and  selecting style, states, icons, and metadata from the variant properties.

components.name: Navigation Rail
components.description:
The rail is a side navigation component that displays three to seven app destinations and, optionally, a floating action button (FAB). Each destination is represented by an icon and label text.

The rail can serve as the sole navigation component on larger screen sizes, such as desktops and tablets.

🛠 Additional options
Segments - Can be edited by selecting individual segments and  selecting states, labels, and badges from the variant properties.

components.name: Plain Tooltip
components.description: Plain tooltips briefly describe a UI element. They're best used for labelling UI elements with no text, like icon-only buttons and fields.

components.name: Progress indicator / circular-determinate
components.description: Determinate indicators display how long a process will take. They should be used when the process completion rate can be detected.

components.name: Progress indicator / circular-indeterminate
components.description: Indeterminate indicators express an unspecified amount of wait time. They should be used when progress isn’t detectable, or if it’s not necessary to indicate how long an activity will take.

components.name: Progress indicator / linear-determinate
components.description: Determinate indicators display how long a process will take. They should be used when the process completion rate can be detected.

components.name: Progress indicator / linear-indeterminate
components.description: Determinate indicators display how long a process will take. They should be used when the process completion rate can be detected.

components.name: Radio buttons
components.description: Radio buttons are the recommended way to allow users to make a single selection from a list of options.

components.name: Rich Tooltip
components.description:
Rich tooltips provide additional context about a UI element. They can optionally contain a subhead, buttons, and hyperlinks.


Rich tooltips are best used for longer text like definitions or explanations.

components.name: Search bar
components.description: Search allows users to enter a keyword or phrase and get relevant information. It’s an alternative to other forms of navigation.

components.name: Search view full-screen
components.description:
Search allows users to enter a keyword or phrase and get relevant information. It’s an alternative to other forms of navigation.
––
Additional options 
List items - Change list items by selecting the individual list item and editing the variant properties.

components.name: Search view modal
components.description:
Search allows users to enter a keyword or phrase and get relevant information. It’s an alternative to other forms of navigation.
––
Additional options 
List items - Change list items by selecting the individual list item and editing the variant properties.

variants.name: Label text=Input text, Show list items=No
Label text=Supporting text, Show list items=No
Label text=Input text, Show list items=Yes
Label text=Supporting text, Show list items=Yes

components.name: Segmented button/5/0
components.description: A set of mutually exclusive button options presented as a single segmented control.
components.usage: Use segmented buttons to switch between related views or filter a dataset when options are limited (2-4 items). Each segment functions as a toggle that deselects other options, making selection state immediately visible. Prefer this over radio buttons when visual prominence and quick switching are priorities.
components.never: Don't use for navigation between pages — use Link or TabBar instead. Segmented buttons are for state selection within a view, not routing.

components.name: Side Sheet
components.description:
Side sheets are surfaces containing supplementary content or actions to support tasks as part of a flow. They are typically anchored on the right edge of larger screens for tablets and desktops. 

There are two types of side sheets: standard and modal.

components.name: Slider / Centered
components.description: Centered sliders have the starting value in the middle of the slider.

components.name: Slider / Continuous
components.description: Continuous sliders can be freely dragged to any value in the range. A slider can be either continuous or discrete.

components.name: Slider / Discrete
components.description: Discrete sliders snap to predefined values, marked with stop indicators. A slider can be either continuous or discrete.

components.name: Slider / Range Selection
components.description: Range selection sliders have two handles that set the minimum and maximum of a range.

components.name: Small FAB
components.description:
Use Floating action buttons (FABs) for the most important action (primary action) on a screen. The FAB appears in front of all other content on screen, and is recognizable for its rounded shape and icon in the center.

A small FAB is used for a secondary, supporting action, or in place of a default FAB on smaller screens.

components.name: Snackbar
components.description: Snackbars inform users of a process that an app has performed or will perform. They appear temporarily, towards the bottom of the screen. They shouldn’t interrupt the user experience, and they don’t require user input to disappear.

components.name: Suggestion chip
components.description: Suggestion chips help narrow a user’s intent by presenting dynamically generated suggestions

components.name: Switch
components.description: Switches are the preferred way to adjust settings. They're used to control binary options – think On/Off or True/False.

components.name: Tabs
components.description:
Tabs organize and allow navigation between groups of content that are related and at the same level of hierarchy.


🛠 Additional options
Tab - Select the state of tabs by selecting the individual tab and editing the variant properties.

components.name: Text field
components.description:
Use a text field when someone needs to enter text into a UI, such as filling in contact or payment information.

🛠 Additional options
Support text - you can toggle supporting text between hide and show.

components.name: Top app bar
components.description: Use a top app bar to provide content and actions related to a current screen, such as navigation, screen headlines, and actions.

components.name: User images/User Images
components.description: A component that displays user identity through circular images in three presentation modes: photographic avatars, initials-based monograms, and validation check states.
components.usage: Use avatars for authenticated users with profile images, monograms when images are unavailable or for initials-based identification, and check variants to indicate verified or completed user states. Select the variant based on available user data and the context's emphasis on verification or identity confirmation.
components.never: Don't use as a clickable button element — wrap in a separate button component if interaction is required, as this maintains semantic HTML and prevents confusion between decorative and interactive elements.
